blurb: The rules in this category are known in Pali as pāṭidesanīya, “entailing acknowledgement”. There are four such rules for the monks, none of which are shared with the nuns. What distinguishes these rules from the pācittiyas is the manner of confession. For these rules the confession is done using a unique formula, with the second rule being the only one in the Monastic Code where all the offenders confess together as a group.

children.translated_name: 1. The training rule on receiving a nun’s food
children.blurb: A monk who receives food from a nun must acknowledge the offense.

children.translated_name: 2. Letting a nun give directions to donors
children.blurb: A monk at an invitational meal who does not tell a nun to stop directing the distribution of food must acknowledge the offense.

children.translated_name: 3. Receiving food from a designated family
children.blurb: A monk who receives food from anyone designated as “in training” must acknowledge the offense.

children.translated_name: 4. Receiving food in a dangerous place
children.blurb: A monk who receives food in a dangerous wilderness dwelling must acknowledge the offense.
